projects.title: Multi-Cloud Solution Architecture
projects.tagline: Flexera One across AWS, Azure and GCP — Infrastructure & SAM governance at global scale — Cognizant
projects.challenge: Multiple global enterprise accounts, license sprawl across three clouds, and no unified view connecting what was bought, what was deployed, and what it cost. Each account solved SAM differently, which meant none of them solved it durably. The need was a single, repeatable architecture that could scale across accounts without starting from zero each time.
architecture: Implemented Flexera One, FNMS and SaaS Manager as the standard SAM/HAM stack across global accounts
Governed CMDB integrations with SAP ERP, Active Directory, O365, Salesforce, Oracle and Citrix for asset-to-financial traceability
Defined the delivery playbook as Competency Charter Lead — architecture standards, quality controls, governance frameworks
Trained and mentored 10+ engineers per account across multi-region delivery models
projects.lessons: Asset management succeeds on cadence, not heroics. The one-off cleanups mattered less than the weekly reclamation rhythm — and a written playbook meant every new account started at maturity, not at zero.

projects.title: Flexera One Cloud Migration
projects.tagline: License intelligence moved from on-prem to AWS — Commonwealth Bank of Australia
projects.challenge: The on-prem FlexNet Manager estate was a scaling and maintenance burden, and cloud spend was outgrowing the governance built for datacenter licensing. The bank needed its license intelligence platform in the cloud without losing a single entitlement record on the way.
architecture: Directed the Flexera One / FNMS on-prem to AWS migration end to end — platform, data and operating model
Established cloud license governance: entitlement tracking, consumption baselines and optimisation cadence
Wired inventory feeds from Tanium, SCCM and BigFix into the Flexera Data Platform normalization backbone
Extended visibility to SaaS spend through Flexera SaaS Manager
projects.lessons: A platform migration is a governance project wearing an infrastructure costume. Moving FNMS was the easy half — rebuilding the license operating model for cloud consumption was where the savings actually came from.

projects.title: Enterprise CMDB Governance
projects.tagline: One trusted configuration model across cloud, server and endpoint — Commonwealth Bank of Australia
projects.challenge: At bank scale, an untrusted CMDB is a risk multiplier: impact analysis guesses, change governance rubber-stamps, and audits crawl. CI data across cloud, server and endpoint estates was inconsistent, ownership was undefined, and dependency mapping existed mostly on whiteboards.
architecture: Defined CI lifecycle standards, CI ownership models and data quality controls as enterprise policy, not team convention
Ran structured audits with reconciliation and normalization cycles across cloud, server and endpoint assets
Led ITOM Discovery integrations for automated CI population and service dependency mapping
Drove cross-team adoption of relationship mapping so impact analysis and change governance ran on real topology
projects.lessons: Data ownership is a political problem wearing a technical costume. The reconciliation rules take weeks; agreeing who owns server records takes months. Start the ownership conversations on day one.

projects.title: Rapid ILMT Audit Defence
projects.tagline: CAD 1.4M saved for a Canadian airport — Wipro
projects.challenge: An IBM audit was inbound and the client had no ILMT deployment — which means full-capacity licensing on every core, at list price. Sub-capacity eligibility required ILMT evidence, and every week without it widened the exposure.
architecture: Rapid IBM BigFix + ILMT deployment across the estate on an audit-driven timeline
Software inventory normalized and reconciled through Flexera against entitlements
Effective License Position (ELP) built and defended with CIO/CFO stakeholders
Audit-readiness governance installed so the position stayed defensible after the engagement
projects.lessons: In licensing, speed is literally money: every unmeasured week of sub-capacity eligibility is list-price exposure. Rapid deployment beat elegant deployment, and the governance came right behind it.

projects.title: Endpoint Compliance at Bank Scale
projects.tagline: Tanium + SCCM governance for ~88% patching SLA — Commonwealth Bank of Australia
projects.challenge: Enterprise endpoint compliance across a bank's estate is a moving target: thousands of endpoints, two management planes, and a patching SLA that regulators actually read. Manual provisioning and patching workflows were burning engineer hours and still missing windows.
architecture: Dual-plane endpoint governance: Tanium for real-time visibility and control, SCCM/MECM for lifecycle management
One compliance policy layer above both tools, so agents could never disagree about the target state
Automated provisioning, monitoring and patching workflows end to end
Compliance telemetry rolled into SLA dashboards and the RCA/PIR governance cycle
projects.lessons: Two endpoint tools means two opinions unless the policy layer is the single source of truth. We stopped arguing about agent counts the day compliance was defined once, above both tools.

projects.title: Observability & AIOps Strategy
projects.tagline: From reactive monitoring to 45–50% faster recovery — Commonwealth Bank of Australia
projects.challenge: Monitoring was reactive: incidents were detected by users before dashboards, Sev1/Sev2 pressure was constant, and every post-incident review found the same root cause — nobody saw it coming because nothing was watching the right layer.
architecture: Defined the cloud observability strategy on Observe Inc. with an OpenTelemetry (OTEL) collection framework
SolarWinds retained for infrastructure monitoring, unified into SLA-driven proactive alerting
Integrated PagerDuty for real-time alerting and on-call escalation paths
Closed the loop with RCA/PIR governance and CMDB-driven impact analysis from the ATLAS programme
projects.lessons: Observability without CMDB context is just noise with better graphs. The telemetry only became actionable when every alert could answer 'which service, whose change, what impact' — that context lives in the configuration model.
